coredata.dc:title: Green preparation of ginger-derived carbon dots accelerates wound healing
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jianting
Fu, Wenjuan
Zhang, Xiangying
Zhang, Qijia
Ma, Dandan
Wang, Yuting
Qian, Wenhui
Zhu, Dong
coredata.dc:description: Inflammation is thought to impact the wound healing pathology and is highly related to many chronic wounds which do not heal. Natural derived herbs with anti-inflammation effects have gained attention with their availability and affordability for clinical treatment. In this study, the ginger (Zingiber officinale) -derived carbon dots (GCDs) were prepared by a green, fast, low-cost microwave-assisted hydrothermal approach at 180 °C by using ginger aqueous as the only precursor without any organic reagent or surface passivation agents. The GCDs showed a small size distribution with the mean particle size of 2.3 nm, enable emit intense blue photoluminescence and exhibited good biocompatibility. The GCDs was successfully applied in a bio-imaging in vitro. Accelerating wound healing by high anti-inflammatory activity was verified both in vivo and in vitro. Moreover, the GCDs effectively blocked the toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4)-mediated nuclear factor-kappa B (NFκB) pathway to significantly dephosphorylate IκBα and inhibited the nuclear translocation of p65 protein, which reduced the expression of proinflammatory factors. On the mRNA levels, tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), interleukin-1β (IL-1β), interleukin-6 (IL-6) and Nitric oxide (NO) were decreased, and the inhibition rate were 51.6%, 81.7%, 86.0%, and 58.7%, respectively, all of which indicated excellent anti-inflammatory effect of the GCDs. Therefore, fluorescent GCDs have a promising application as an imaging tool and functional wound dressing for wound clinical management. The study also brings a new idea for the discovery of effective special substances that is not limited to bioactive small-molecule compounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave hydrothermal
Ginger-derived carbon dots
Inflammation
Wound healing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro biocompatibility and anti-inflammatory assays
In vivo anti-inflammatory and wound healing assays

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation of the GCDs; (b) The anti-inflammatory mechanism of the GCDs in wound healing. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The Characterizations of the GCDs (a) TEM image; (b) Particle size distribution of the GCDs; (c) FTIR spectra of the GCDs; (d) UV–vis and FL spectra of the GCDs; (e) The full XPS survey spectrum of the GCDs; (f) C1s spectra of the GCDs; (g) O1s spectra of the GCDs; (h) N1s spectra of the GCDs; (i) XRD spectrum of the GCDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Relative cell viability of the RAW264.7 cells incubated with the GCDs for 24 h; (b) Relative cell viability of the HUVEC cells incubated with the GCDs for 24 h; (c) Relative expression level of TNF; (d) Relative expression level of IL-6; (e) Relative expression level of IL-1β; (f) Relative expression level of NO; (g) The effect of the GCDs on the HUVEC cell migration (scale bar:1 mm); (h) Scratch migration rate of the GCDs on the HUVEC cells for 12 h; (i) Scratch migration rate of the GCDs on the HUVEC cells for 24 h *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Western blotting of TLR4, p-IκBα, IκBα, P65, p-P65 expression in the LPS treated RAW264.7 inflammatory model; (b) Relative expression of TLR4 proteins; (c) Relative expression of p-p65 proteins compared to p65 proteins; (d) Relative expression of p-IκB proteins compared to IKB proteins; (e) Cell imaging in the RAW264.7. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01 and ***P < 0.001. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

In vivo wound experimental analysis (a) Wound images of the GCDs-treated rats and the untreated rats on day 3, 7, 11, and 14; (b) On day 3, day 7, day 11, and day 14, the wound contraction rate of the GCDs-treated rats and untreated rats; (c) Relative expression level of the NO; (d) The HE analysis: purple arrows indicate squamous epithelium; black arrows indicate inflammatory infiltration; yellow arrows indicate white blood cells exudate a large number of blood scabs formed by platelet fibrin coagulation; red arrows indicate newborn capillaries and hyperplastic fibroblasts (scale bars = 100 μm). *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Green preparation of ginger-derived carbon dots accelerates wound healing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 210023, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210023, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inflammation is thought to impact the wound healing pathology and is highly related to many chronic wounds which do not heal. Natural derived herbs with anti-inflammation effects have gained attention with their availability and affordability for clinical treatment. In this study, the ginger (Zingiber officinale) -derived carbon dots (GCDs) were prepared by a green, fast, low-cost microwave-assisted hydrothermal approach at 180 °C by using ginger aqueous as the only precursor without any organic reagent or surface passivation agents. The GCDs showed a small size distribution with the mean particle size of 2.3 nm, enable emit intense blue photoluminescence and exhibited good biocompatibility. The GCDs was successfully applied in a bio-imaging in vitro. Accelerating wound healing by high anti-inflammatory activity was verified both in vivo and in vitro. Moreover, the GCDs effectively blocked the toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4)-mediated nuclear factor-kappa B (NFκB) pathway to significantly dephosphorylate IκBα and inhibited the nuclear translocation of p65 protein, which reduced the expression of proinflammatory factors. On the mRNA levels, tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), interleukin-1β (IL-1β), interleukin-6 (IL-6) and Nitric oxide (NO) were decreased, and the inhibition rate were 51.6%, 81.7%, 86.0%, and 58.7%, respectively, all of which indicated excellent anti-inflammatory effect of the GCDs. Therefore, fluorescent GCDs have a promising application as an imaging tool and functional wound dressing for wound clinical management. The study also brings a new idea for the discovery of effective special substances that is not limited to bioactive small-molecule compounds.

ce:section.ce:para: Skin is the most important barrier against external invasion to maintain a stable internal environment in the human body. Repair of barrier function depends on rapid wound healing [1]. Abnormal wound healing can lead to the breakdown of the body's immune function, causing severe bleeding, hyperplasia of the scar, bacterial invasion, and even death [2]. Wound healing procedures include: hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, and remodeling, which are consecutive but overlapped [3], which is highly related to the response of inflammatory the cells and the release of various growth factors and cytokines from the wound site [4]. Some chronic wounds, such as those caused by diabetes, aging, and infection showed an extended period of healing [5], which always remained in the self-perpetuating phase of inflammation and was difficult to enter an orderly healing phase [6–9]. The restraint of exceeding inflammation could accelerate resolution of the inflammation period and initiate proliferation and remodeling phase. Therefore, an effective approach to suppressing inflammation is key to resolving wound healing processes, especially for those chronic wounds. Kinds of anti-inflammatory bioactive substances from plants such as aloe vera [10] and Rhein [11] exhibited promising effects in wound healing.

Carbonic herb, a charcoal-processed product of Chinese medicinal herbs by a high temperature procedure based on traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) theory, has been employed as a convergent hemostasis agent for nearly 900 years, firstly recorded in the Chinese Song Dynasty (960 AD) [12]. A new kind of substance, carbon dots (CDs), were found in an aqueous solution of charred herbal extracts [14,15], enabling them to be used as hemostatic therapy agents. CDs are emerging class of “zero-dimensional” carbon-based substance with attractive properties, including biocompatibility [13], superior water dispersion [14], low cytotoxicity [15]. Liu et al. obtained CDs to exert hemostatic effect by activating the fibrinogen system from golden cypress aqueous extracts, which have excellent stability, suitable for long-term preservation [16]. Cheng found a Junci Medulla Carbonisata-derived CDs (JMC-CDs) in JMC extracts, which inhibit thrombocytopenia and local bleeding via activation of the fibrinogen system and in vitro coagulation pathway [17]. Yan et al. isolated and discovered a new water-soluble CDs (PTC-CDs) from the aqueous solution of Pollen Typhae Carbonisata (PTC), and the fingerprint map showed PTC-CDs do not contain proto-precursor substances and exert hemostatic efficacy by stimulating the endogenous coagulation system and activating the fibrinogen system [18]. Wang et al. prepared Mulberry silkworm cocoon CDs (MSC-CDs) by a modified pyrolysis method that exhibited marked anti-inflammatory bioactivity [19]. As a rhizome plant, ginger carbon dots were researched in antibacterial activity [20], sensors [21], photoluminescence [22],etc. Ginger was widely used in TCM. And ginger charcoal, made by drying fresh ginger slices and then frying them at a high temperature, is often applied in hemostasis for the treatment of wounds. At present, the application of ginger quantum dots in wound healing has not been studied and few researches have explored the pharmacodynamic mechanism of natural product-derived CDs. Moreover, most of the preparation approaches for CDs require more than 10 h and a high temperature above 200 °C. Therefore, faster and more efficient preparation methods should be proposed.

Herein, we demonstrated a green, fast, and low-cost microwave-assisted hydrothermal approach to preparing CDs at 180 °C by using ginger aqueous as the only precursor. The morphology, composition, and optical properties of the ginger-derived CDs (GCDs) were investigated. Compared with the traditional preparation method, this method significantly reduces the preparation time and increases efficiency without using any organic reagents or surface passivation agents. The GCDs were applied in the bio-imaging in inflammatory model cells, lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced RAW264.7 cells. Most of the GCDs were distributed in the cytoplasm, and a small part of GCDs were existed in the nucleus due to them ultra-small size, which allowed them to reach the nucleus through nuclear pores. The GCDs enabled accelerated wound healing by a high anti-inflammatory activity through effectively blocking the TLR4/NF-κB inflammatory trigger pathway and effectively reducing damage caused by inflammatory mediators. Also, the GCDs were found to greatly reduce the occurrence of inflammatory infiltration during wound healing and accelerate wound healing. As far as we know, this study firstly demonstrated the efficient anti-inflammatory effect of ginger-derived CDs, which were prepared by a faster and more efficient method, applied to wound healing, and showed strong fluorescence while treating diseases, which assisted us in observing the distribution of drugs entering cells. Moreover, we demonstrated that the anti-inflammatory effect of GCDs is related to the TLR4/NFκB pathway. Therefore, the fluorescent GCDs with high anti-inflammation responsiveness could be employed as an imaging tool and an outstanding functional wound dressing for wound clinical treatment.

Dried ginger was obtained from Luxi, Yunnan (China). Twenty-five male SD (Sprague Dawley) rats, weighing 180–210 g, were purchased from Hangzhou Medical College (Hangzhou, China). All the researches were in conformity with the NIH (National Institutes of Health) guidelines for the Guide of the Care (No.202106A031).

Dried ginger slices were ground into powder, and 0.5 g of ginger powder was putted into 20 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated for 30 min. In the microwave synthesizer (CEM), the mixture was processed for 1 h at 180 °C, then cooled to the room temperature (25 °C). After the supernatant containing GCDs of the reaction solution was pretreated by 0.22 μm membrane filtration and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) every 8 h to change the external solution of the dialysis bag, dialysis for 3 days for further purification. The purified carbon dots solution was placed in a freeze dryer for freeze-drying for 24 h to obtain a carbon dot powder. The quantum yield of fluorescence emission was calculated. Quinine sulfate (0.05 mol L−1 H2SO4) was used as the standard reference material, and its quantum yield was 0.54. The quantum yield of GCDs was calculated by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensity of GCDs and quinine sulfate. The absorbance of the two solutions at the corresponding excitation wavelength should not exceed 0.1 to eliminate the reabsorption effect [23].

High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were captured on a Tecnai-G2-F20 (FEI) with an accelerating voltage of 300 kV. The possible functional groups were analyzed by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo). X-ray Diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy was obtained by the XRD-6100 (Shimadzu). Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectra were performed by a multifunctional microplate reader (Tecan), and recorded at the full scan absorption in the range of 200 nm–800 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) examination of elements of GCDs was used for a ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo). The Zeta potential of carbon dots was recorded using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. The imaging of cells was observed by a DMI8 Inverted Fluorescent Microscope (Leica).

The human umbilical vein endothelial (HUVEC) cells and mouse monocyte - macrophage leukemia (RAW.264.7) cells were cultivated for 24 h after being added at a density of 8000 cells per well of the 96-well plate. Then, the original medium was discarded, the GCDs were added to the wells and grown with them for 24 h at a concentration of 0–1000 μg mL−1200 μL of CCK-8 (1:10, Vazyme) was added and replaced the original medium. After 2 h, the absorbance at 450 nm was read by Tecan.

RAW264.7 and HUVEC cells were collected, when the cell viability was over 90% by trypan blue counting. The cell concentration in the suspension was adjusted to 1 × 105. After fully mixing, 1 mL of the cell solution was put into the confocal dish and incubated overnight. The medium containing LPS (1 μg mL−1) was replaced with the original medium. After 4 h incubation, the GCDs (200 μg mL−1) were incubated in the cell incubator for 4 h. Drug-containing media was discarded, and fixed the well with 1 mL of paraformaldehyde for 20 min. After three times of PBS cleaning, the cell surface was covered with 300 μL of Propidium Iodide (PI) and stained for 5 min. After PBS cleaning, 300 μL of anti-fluorescence quenching agent was added to avoid light at 4 °C.

The expression of mRNA level related indicators was detected by qPCR method. Then the RNA concentration was calculated, reverse transcription of RNA into cDNA and then perform the qPCR reaction using the instrument (Bio-Rad). Primer sequences are shown in Fig. S1. 2−ΔΔCt method for determining the expression of inflammatory factors at the RNA level [24].

After the cells were fully washed off the original medium, the protein concentration was determined using the bicinchoninic acid (BCA) kit after 150 μL of protein lysate had been added. Sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) electrophoresis was used to separate the protein, after which it was transferred and treated with a primary antibody overnight at 4 °C. All the antibodies are from Cell Signaling Technology (CST). The Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF) was fully washed to remove residual primary antibody with tris buffered saline with Tween 20 (TBST), then was incubated for 2 h at room temperature with the secondary antibody [25]. After three times of full washing, bands were exposed after incubation with the enhanced chemiluminescence substrate kit for 1 min.

The plates were pre-marked at the bottom, and then a concentration of 50,000 cells were put into each well for a 24-h incubation. The homogeneous scratch in the middle of the six-well plate were created by a 200 μL tip. The HUVEC cells were treated by GCDs with doses of 100 and 200 μg mL−1, and photographed at 0, 12, and 24h after the treatment [26].

Twenty-five clean grade SD male rats, weighing 180–210 g, above 6–8 weeks old maintained at 18–25 °C and 50% constant humidity. The hair on the rat's back was carefully removed and disinfected by iodophor. One mouse was raised in each cage after modeling. Animals are anesthetized by inhaling of isoflurane. On either side of the back, there are two full-thickness skin wounds 1 × 1 cm2 in diameter [27,28]. The rats in the experiment group were dripped with 200 μg mL−1 GCDs solution on the wound surface once daily, and the control group received an equal volume of deionized water. All rat wounds were photographed at 0, 3, 7, 11, and 14 days.

All rat wounds were photographed every three days, and the wounds were analyzed by Image J software. W
0
is referred to as the original wound area, while Wt is referred to as the remaining wound area. The formula for wound healing rate is as follows [27]:
W %
=
(
W 0
−
W t
)
/
W 0
/ × 100
%

The fresh tissue blocks of the animals were infiltrated with paraformaldehyde (4%) and ethanol-induced dehydration. The transparent tissue blocks were set in molten paraffin and sliced into 4–8 μm thick pieces for HE staining. The stained sections were fully washed and then coated with gum. A 100 × magnification microscope was used to examine the histological structure.

The average diameter of the GCDs is 2.3 nm by HRTEM and Digital Micrograph software analysis (Fig. 2 (a and b)), and the lattice spacing is above 0.212 nm (Fig. 2 (a)), which is consistent with the CDs in the previous report [29]. This ultra-small size enables facilitation cellular uptake across the physiological barrier. The results of the FT-IR spectroscopy demonstrated that the surface of the GCDs contains a number of reactive groups, such as –OH, –COOH, and –CONH2, which exploited excellent hydrophilicity and good dispersion in water (Fig. 2 (c)). While the excitation wavelength was 360 nm, the fluorescence emission reaches the maximum, and the GCDs enable the production of blue fluorescence with a wavelength of 430 nm, and the Stokes shift is 70 nm (Fig. 2 (d), Fig. S1). And the quantum yield of GCDs is 6.0%. As shown in (Fig. S2), the GCDs showed negatively charged nanoparticles with a potential of −25 mV in water, since plenty of active groups presented onto the surface, resulting in good dispersibility, stability, and biocompatibility of the GCDs [30]. Additionally, XPS was used to examine the ultimate composition of the GCDs' surface. Three major peaks for C (74.85%), O (21.16%) and N (3.99%) were found in the spectrum, as shown in (Fig. 2 (e)). The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2 (f)) confirmed the presence of C–C/C=C (284.4 eV), C=N/C=O (285.1 eV), C–O/C–N (286.2 eV), and C=O (288.6 eV). The peaks at 533.1 and 531.8 eV belonged to C–O and C=O (Fig. 2 (g)), The N 1s spectra (Fig. 2 (h)) also show the presence of C=N and C–NH bonds, which correspond to the peaks at 399.6 and 400.6 eV. The XRD spectrogram of the GCDs reveals a wide reflection peak at the center at 20.6°, which is in accord with the (002) diffraction facets of graphitic carbon attributed to disordered carbon atoms (Fig. 2 (i)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biocompatibility and anti-inflammatory assays
ce:section.ce:para: As a potential therapeutic agent for anti-inflammation, the biocompatibility of the GCDs is firstly critical for their further biomedical applications. The cytotoxicity of the GCDs was studied in RAW264.7 cells and HUVEC cells by using CCK-8 kit assays. When the GCD concentration rose from 0 to 1000 μg mL−1, there was no obvious toxicity in contrast to the control group. (Fig. 3 (a,b)), and most concentration groups have better cell viability than the control group. We also compared the aqueous solution of dried ginger without hydrothermal treatment and found that it showed cytotoxicity at 800 μg mL −1 (Fig. S3. (a,b)). These results clearly indicate that the prepared GCDs have good biosafety and the potential to promoting cell growth. A typical inflammatory model was fabricated with LPS-induced RAW264.7 cells, in which the mRNA levels of TNF-α (Fig. 3 (c)), IL-1β (Fig. 3 (d)), IL-6 (Fig. 3 (e)) and NO (Fig. 3 (f)) were increased to 6.02 times, 268 times, 202 times and 4.48 times, respectively, demonstrated the successful fabrication of inflammatory model. The group added with the GCDs at 100 μg mL−1 dose greatly decreased the expression, and the inhibition rates were 37.1%, 62.8%, 57.5%, and 32.8% of inflammatory factors in TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and nitric oxide (NO), compared to the LPS group without the GCDs. With the increase of the GCD's concentration to 200 μg mL−1, a higher anti-inflammatory activity was observed, and the pro-inflammatory mediators were decreased, and the inhibition rates were 51.6%, 81.7%, 86.0%, and 58.7%. The model group added with the GCDs enabled significantly reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory mediators compared with the LPS group, and had a great anti-inflammatory potential in inflammatory wounds. The GCDs also enhanced the migration capacity of the HUVEC cells at concentrations for 12 h and 24 h; the cell scratch closure rate was 39.8% when cells were cultured at 100 μg mL−1 of the GCDs for 24 h, which exceeded the level of the control group at 20.4%; the cell scratch closure rate was 19.7% and 42.5% when cells were cultured at 200 μg mL−1 of the GCDs within 12 h and 24 h, respectively, which was obviously in comparison to the control group 11.4% and 20.4% (Fig. 3(g–i)). We also investigated the anti-inflammatory effect and cell migration ability between the aqueous solution of dried ginger without hydrothermal treatment and the GCDs. As shown in (Fig. S4. (a,b)) Dried ginger itself has an anti-inflammatory effect and is not statistically different from the GCDs, but the GCDs show better cell migration ability (Fig. S5.).

TLR4 is a transmembrane signal receptor that mainly mediates the transmembrane signal transduction of LPS. After TLR4 binds to ligands, it activates NF-κB, promotes phosphorylation of IκB, activates nuclear translocation of the p65 protein, and finally releases inflammatory factors to cause damage to cells. (Fig. 1(b)). As shown in Fig. 4 (a,b), that the expression of TLR4 protein was increased to 1.34 times in the LPS model group contrast to control group. The p-IκBα protein and p-p65 protein were significantly increased after LPS-induced modeling, and the relative expression of p-IκBα proteins and p-p65 proteins was increased to 2.00 times and 10.9 times with the contrast to control group Fig. 4(a,c,d). However, the cells in the modeling group after the GCDs administration of 200 μg mL−1 concentration were able to noticeably inhibit the expression of TLR4 and block the protein-phosphorylation level of NF-κB, and the relative expression of TLR4 protein, p-IκBα proteins, and p-p65 proteins was decreased, and the inhibition rates were 32.5%, 47.9%, and 80.2% compared to the modeling group. The blocking of the NFκB pathway by GCDs is related to its downregulation of TLR4 protein expression. We considered that this may be due to the fact that GCDs, as nanoparticles, have an ultra-small size that makes it easier for them to penetrate cell membranes and nuclear membranes and be taken up by cells. And we also think that the GCDs has abundant groups, hydrogen bonds can be formed with TLR4 to help them closely bind [31]. In Fig. 4(e), the distribution of GCDs in inflammatory cells was studied. The GCD emit bright blue fluorescence, most of the GCDs were distributed in the cytoplasm and a small size was existed in the nucleus. This may be due to the ultra-small size of the GCDs, which allows them to reach the nucleus through nuclear pores.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo anti-inflammatory and wound healing assays
ce:section.ce:para: The therapeutic impact on rat skin wounds of using the GCDs was investigated. (Fig. 5 (a)). On the 3rd day, the wound healing rate of the group treated with the GCDs of 200 μg mL−1 concentration was 43.9%, while that of the untreated control group was 25.7%. On the 7th day, the wound healing rates of the group treated with the GCDs and the control group were 67.0% and 57.6%, respectively, and were significantly different. On the 11th day, the healing rate of the group treated with the GCDs and the control group was 91.9% and 87.2%, respectively. On the 14th day, the wounds in the GCDs treated group were basically closed, and the wound healing rate was 97.1%. The control group still exhibited obvious scab formation (Fig. 5(b)). The results of the NO experiment showed that the level of NO in the wounds of SD rats after GCDS administration was lower. On the 7th and 14th days, the expression of NO in the GCDs treatment group decreased by 4.82 and 1.93 times compared with the control group, respectively, which further proved the great anti-inflammatory effect of GCDs in vivo (Fig. 5(c)). As shown in Fig. 5(d), the epidermis of the tissue in the GCDs treated group had formed some squamous epithelium, and a large number of new capillaries and granulation tissue formed by proliferating fibroblasts were observed in the deep-layer tissue, and inflammatory infiltration was less. There were more white blood cells seen in the tissue of the control group. The blood scab formed by platelet fibrin aggregation and a large number of obvious inflammatory cell infiltration were observed in the tissue. This observation demonstrated that the GCDs have a superior ability to promote wound healing.

In this study, a green, fast, low-cost microwave-assisted hydrothermal approach was proposed for the efficient and rapid preparation of carbon dots using dried ginger solution as the only raw material. The GCDs, with their ultra-small size and plenty of reactive groups, are highly dispersible in water, and enables greatly improves their bioavailability. This study also revealed the significant anti-inflammatory activity of the GCDs and preliminary explored their mechanism of action, demonstrating that the overexpression of related pro-inflammatory mediators was inhibited by blocking the phosphorylation pathway of TLR4/NF-κB. By using the native fluorescence of the GCDs, the distribution and localization of the anti-inflammation GCDs were observed within the cells during treatment. The results showed that the GCDs effectively promote wound healing and reduce inflammation, making them a highly promising material for topical wound application. This discovery contributes to the pharmacological efficacy of quantum dots while also offering a fresh perspective on the study of Chinese medicine's components.

Jianting Li: Ideas, Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Validation, Methodology, Visualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing e original draft, preparation. Wenjuan Fu: Methodology, Visualization, Investigation. Xiangying Zhang: Methodology, Visualization, Investigation. Qijia Zhang: Formal analysis. Dandan Ma: Formal analysis. Yuting Wang: Formal analysis. Wenhui Qian: Ideas. Dong Zhu: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The study was supported by the Project of National Natural Science Foundation of China 82274219. We also thank the Open Project of Chinese Materia Medica First-Class Discipline of Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine No. 2020YLXK025 and Jiangsu Province Postgraduate Scientific Research Practice and Innovation Plan Project KYCX21_1735 and SJCX21_0679 for support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2023.03.039.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The potential application of natural products in cutaneous wound healing: a review of preclinical evidence

Wound Repair Regen.: Off. Publ. Wound Heal. Soc. Eur. Tissue Repair Soc.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioactive anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, antioxidative silicon-based nanofibrous dressing enables cutaneous tumor photothermo-chemo therapy and infection-induced wound healing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xi, J. Ge, M. Wang, M. Chen, W. Niu, W. Cheng, Y. Xue, C. Lin, B.
